food_subgroup: Japanese rice bowl with assorted raw seafood toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bara Chirashi Don; 'rice bowl food product' (FOODON_00002809) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Sushi) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for bara chirashi don style rice bowls with cubed sashimi.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Japanese rice bowl dish.

preparation_method: raw seafood cubed and arranged over vinegared sushi rice
tamago (Japanese egg omelette) sliced and placed on top
served chilled or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Bara Chirashi Don is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 240 g of sushi rice and 160 g of assorted seafood toppings including cubed salmon, tuna, shrimp, ikura, and tamago, served in a single bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient values estimated by ingredient composition: sushi rice (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169753, Cooked white short-grain rice), salmon raw (USDA FDC ID 175168), tuna raw (USDA FDC ID 175159), shrimp cooked (USDA FDC ID 175180), salmon roe (USDA FDC ID 175170), egg omelette/tamago approximated from USDA FDC ID 173424 (Egg, whole, cooked, omelet). Weighted composite per 100 g of total dish (400 g serving: ~240 g rice, ~50 g salmon, ~40 g tuna, ~30 g shrimp, ~20 g ikura, ~20 g tamago).
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Bara Chirashi Don as a composite dish. Values are computed as a weighted average of USDA FoodData Central entries for each component ingredient, proportioned to the described 400 g serving (240 g sushi rice + 160 g mixed seafood/tamago). Rice vinegar and sugar in sushi rice seasoning add minor carbohydrate and energy contributions. Per-100g values derived from this composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi (white rice with vinegar and fish toppings, GI ~52).
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bara Chirashi Don. The GI of 52 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi (white rice with vinegar seasoning and fish toppings), which is the closest analogous food. Vinegar in sushi rice is documented to lower the glycemic response of white rice (Ostman et al., 2005, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition). The dominant glycemic contributor is the sushi rice component (~60% of serving weight). GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (82 g from 400 g at 20.5 g/100g) / 100 = 42.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 210 mg per 100 g; a full 400 g serving delivers approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 35–42% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2400 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a single serving of Bara Chirashi Don contributes a substantial sodium load, primarily from soy sauce accompaniment and seasoned sushi rice; portion control and requesting reduced-sodium soy sauce are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index of 52 (low GI) but high glycemic load per serving (~42.6) due to the large sushi rice portion; the protein and omega-3 fatty acid content from seafood components may attenuate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should note that while the GI of Bara Chirashi Don is in the low range (GI 52), the glycemic load per standard serving is high due to the substantial rice content; reducing the rice portion or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in marine omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from salmon, tuna, and ikura; low in saturated fat (~0.7 g per 100 g); cholesterol is moderate at approximately 85 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bara Chirashi Don is a cardioprotective meal option due to its high content of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids from multiple oily fish components, which are associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; sodium intake from soy sauce should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bara Chirashi Don is a Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of vinegared sushi rice topped with an assortment of cubed or sliced raw and lightly prepared seafood. The name derives from 'bara' (scattered or cubed pieces), 'chirashi' (scattered), and 'don' (bowl). Unlike traditional chirashi sushi where toppings are artfully arranged, bara chirashi features bite-sized cubed pieces of fish and seafood scattered freely over the rice. A typical serving at a Japanese restaurant in Singapore includes cubed salmon, raw tuna, cooked or raw shrimp, ikura (salmon roe), and tamago (Japanese sweet rolled egg omelette), totalling approximately 160 g of toppings over 240 g of sushi rice in a 400 g bowl. The dish is served chilled or at room temperature. The flavour profile is clean, oceanic, and mildly sweet from the vinegared rice and tamago, with a rich umami depth from the ikura. Nutritionally, Bara Chirashi Don is a high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate meal providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g. It is an excellent source of marine omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from salmon, tuna, and ikura, which support cardiovascular health. The dish has a low glycemic index (~52) due to the acetic acid in sushi rice vinegar, which slows starch digestion, though the glycemic load per full serving is high due to the rice volume. Sodium content is moderate from the seasoned rice but can increase significantly with soy sauce. The dish is not suitable for individuals with fish, shellfish, or egg allergies. It is not halal-certified due to the presence of raw seafood and the typical use of mirin (rice wine) in tamago preparation.

What is ikura in chirashi don?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Bara Chirashi Don as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for individual ingredients (sushi rice, salmon, tuna, shrimp, ikura, tamago) proportioned to the described 400 g serving. GI is estimated from the closest analogous University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries; no single approved-source record for Bara Chirashi Don exists. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or a future HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as seafood and egg ingredients contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fats from fish are below detection threshold (confirmed zero by formulation). 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9) are estimated from USDA component data for salmon, tuna, and shrimp. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database sushi entry; no direct GI measurement for bara chirashi don was found in peer-reviewed literature. 5. Sodium estimate of 210 mg per 100 g reflects seasoned sushi rice only and does not include additional soy sauce served alongside, which would substantially increase sodium intake. 6. The restaurant source (The Oyster Bank - Funan, field survey data) is noted for provenance context only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source. 7. Allergen list includes gluten as soy sauce is typically served alongside; the dish itself does not contain wheat-based ingredients. 8. Halal status is false as mirin (rice wine) is commonly used in tamago preparation and raw seafood handling practices vary. 9. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA component entries used for this composite estimate; these are not confirmed zero values.
